ontology.foodex2_description: Egg yolk, raw — hen egg yolk, unprocessed

serving.natural_language_portion: One large chicken egg yolk weighs approximately 17 g and provides around 55 kcal, making it a common single-serving unit in cooking and baking.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken egg, yolk, raw, fresh — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for chicken egg yolk, raw. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01125 (Egg, yolk, raw, fresh) where HPB does not report fatty acid fractions. Vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content also supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01125.

gi_source.title: N/A — Egg yolk, raw; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible digestible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Egg yolk contains only 3.59 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which the majority is not glycaemic starch. Pure protein-and-fat foods such as egg yolk are not assigned a GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is not applicable; glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (48 mg/100 g); high saturated fat and cholesterol warrant moderation
hypertension.guidance: Egg yolk is low in sodium and does not directly raise blood pressure; however, individuals with hypertension should limit intake to 1–2 yolks per day and avoid high-sodium preparations, as the high saturated fat content (8.72 g/100 g) may contribute to arterial stiffness over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.59 g/100 g) and negligible sugar (0.56 g/100 g); high protein and fat slow gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw egg yolk has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose due to its very low carbohydrate content; it is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, but the high caloric density (322 kcal/100 g) and elevated cholesterol (1085 mg/100 g) require portion control and monitoring of lipid profiles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high cholesterol (1085 mg/100 g) and elevated saturated fat (8.72 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg yolk contains exceptionally high dietary cholesterol (1085 mg/100 g), which exceeds the 300 mg/day threshold in a single large yolk; individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should limit consumption to 2–4 yolks per week and consult a clinician, as saturated fat and dietary cholesterol together may raise LDL-C in susceptible individuals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (8.72 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; no qualifying anti-inflammatory MUFA/PUFA carve-out applies
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Egg yolk's saturated fat content may promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6); individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit to 1 yolk per meal and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as leafy greens or omega-3-rich ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken egg yolk, raw, is the lipid-rich inner portion of a fresh hen's egg, carefully separated from the albumen (egg white) before any cooking or heat treatment. It presents as a dense, golden-yellow, semi-viscous liquid with a rich, creamy, and slightly sulphurous flavour and a smooth, unctuous texture that coats the palate. Raw egg yolk is a foundational culinary ingredient used globally in emulsified sauces such as mayonnaise, hollandaise, and Caesar dressing; in pasta dishes such as carbonara; in custards, crème brûlée, and ice cream bases; and as a natural binding and emulsifying agent in baking and confectionery. The yolk is the primary source of lecithin (phosphatidylcholine) in the egg, which accounts for its powerful emulsifying properties. Nutritionally, raw egg yolk is one of the most nutrient-dense single-ingredient foods available: per 100 g it provides 322 kcal, 15.86 g protein, 26.54 g total fat (of which 8.72 g is saturated, 11.74 g monounsaturated, and 3.97 g polyunsaturated), and only 3.59 g carbohydrate with 0.56 g sugar. It is an excellent source of fat-soluble vitamins A (381 mcg RAE), D (3.31 mcg), and E (2.58 mg), and provides significant B-vitamins including riboflavin (0.53 mg), B12 (1.95 mcg), and folate (146 mcg DFE). Mineral content is notable: phosphorus (390 mg), iron (2.73 mg), zinc (2.30 mg), calcium (129 mg), and selenium (56 mcg). Sodium is very low at 48 mg/100 g, making it appropriate for low-sodium dietary patterns. Clinically, the exceptionally high dietary cholesterol (1085 mg/100 g) is the dominant concern for cardiovascular and dyslipidaemic patients, and saturated fat at 8.72 g/100 g exceeds thresholds relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular risk. Conversely, the very low carbohydrate content makes egg yolk well-suited for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible digestible carbohydrate content. This record covers raw, separated egg yolk only; cooked preparations are separate records.
common_user_queries: How much cholesterol is in an egg yolk?
Is egg yolk good for keto diet?
How many calories in one egg yolk?
Is egg yolk high in protein?
Can I eat egg yolk if I have high blood pressure?
What vitamins are in egg yolk?
Is egg yolk bad for heart disease?
How much saturated fat is in egg yolk?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (exact match for chicken egg yolk, raw) with confidence 0.95. Fatty acid fractions and micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01125 (exact match). Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore database; egg yolk contains no plant-based fibre. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central NDB 01125. Vitamin C is set to 0.0 g/100 g — USDA FoodData Central NDB 01125 reports 0 mg for egg yolk, raw; this is a confirmed zero value as egg yolk contains no meaningful vitamin C. Saturated fat (8.72 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (11.74 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated fat (3.97 g/100 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01125 (Egg, yolk, raw, fresh) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions separately. Glycemic index is not applicable: egg yolk is a high-fat, high-protein food with only 3.59 g carbohydrate per 100 g; no GI value is assigned in the University of Sydney GI Database for egg yolk. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0. Cholesterol at 1085 mg/100 g is exceptionally high and is the primary clinical concern for cardiovascular and dyslipidaemic patients. This record covers raw, separated egg yolk only; cooked preparations (fried, boiled, scrambled) are separate records.

Vitamin C is now correctly set to 0.0 with confirmed-zero documentation. Glycemic index is null with gi_category not_applicable per the low-carbohydrate food rule. LLM description is comprehensive at approximately 1,650 characters. All nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore (exact match) and USDA FoodData Central NDB 01125 (supplementary fatty acids and micronutrients). All three mandatory health conditions present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are defensible. Aliases are consistent between flat array and aliases_by_language.
